common_name: Variegated Shell Ginger (Cream-and-Green Striped Tropical Ginger Specimen)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants after the last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Variegated Shell Ginger prefers afternoon shade protection — full-day direct sun can cause the cream variegation to bleach to white and the leaf margins to burn in Gulf South summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Variegated Shell Ginger is one of the most spectacular foliage plants for Gulf South tropical garden designs — a large, bold ornamental with broad, strap-shaped leaves (12–18 inches long) brilliantly streaked with alternating bands of deep green and creamy-white to ivory-yellow variegation that creates one of the most visually striking striped foliage effects of any commonly available ornamental plant. The creamy-white striping covers 30–50% of each leaf surface in irregular, painterly bands that vary in width and distribution, creating a luminous effect in partial shade where the cream bands glow against the dark-green background. In spring or early summer, mature established clumps produce pendant clusters of shell-shaped white flowers with yellow and red markings — beautiful but secondary to the spectacular foliage display. The variegated form is significantly more ornamental than the solid-green Alpinia zerumbet (shell ginger) but slightly slower-growing and more heat-sensitive — the cream portions of the variegated leaves are more vulnerable to sun scorch than the all-green type. In zone 9, Variegated Shell Ginger is a perennial that may be top-killed by hard freezes but returns from the rhizome in spring — heavy mulching protects the roots through zone 9a winters. The bold, tropical scale and spectacular variegation make this an outstanding specimen plant for partially shaded entryways, courtyard gardens, and tropical-theme garden designs.

common_name: Golden Angel's Trumpet (Large Pendulous Yellow-Trumpet Brugmansia Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; all parts of Brugmansia are toxic if ingested — plant away from children's play areas and avoid contact with sap; wash hands after pruning.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Golden Queen is a yellow-flowered Brugmansia (angel's trumpet) producing the spectacular large-scale tropical display characteristic of the genus — very large (10–14 inch), pendulous, trumpet-shaped flowers in warm golden-yellow hanging in clusters from the branch tips, releasing an extraordinary heavy fragrance particularly intense in the evening hours. The golden-yellow flower color of 'Golden Queen' is distinctly different from the white, peach, pink, and apricot tones of other angel's trumpet varieties — a warm, pure yellow that creates a dramatic tropical focal point in partial shade. In zone 9, Brugmansia grows as a large tropical shrub or small tree reaching 6–12 feet, blooming most heavily in spring and fall as temperatures moderate from summer extremes. The plant requires generous water and fertility to produce its finest flower display — large containers require daily watering in summer. All parts of Brugmansia are toxic if ingested and should be handled with caution; the sap can irritate skin and eyes. In zone 9b and 10, Golden Queen grows as a perennial. In zone 9a, frost may kill the top growth but the plant often returns from the roots with mulch protection; container-grown specimens can be moved indoors before frost. The spectacular flowers and fragrance make angel's trumpets one of the most dramatic ornamentals for Gulf South tropical garden designs.

common_name: White Duranta (White-Flowered Golden Dewdrop Shrub with Golden Berries)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; the golden-yellow berries of Duranta are toxic if eaten by humans and pets — plant away from areas where berry consumption is likely.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; White Duranta ('Alba') is the white-flowered form of golden dewdrop (Duranta erecta) — a widely grown tropical shrub native to the Americas that produces arching, cascading branches of small, white flowers (rather than the blue-purple of the species) followed by the showy strings of golden-yellow berries that give the plant its common name 'golden dewdrop'. The white flower and golden berry combination creates an elegant, clean color scheme distinct from the blue-and-gold of standard Duranta. Duranta is one of the most important butterfly and hummingbird plants for Gulf South gardens — the flowers attract monarch butterflies, queen butterflies, sulphur butterflies, and a wide range of native bees throughout the long bloom season (summer through fall), making it an outstanding pollinator plant in full-sun borders and along fence lines. The golden-yellow berry clusters persist through fall and are consumed by mockingbirds, catbirds, and other frugivorous birds. In zone 9, Duranta grows as a perennial shrub in protected locations, often killed to the ground by hard freezes but returning vigorously from the roots in spring. The fast-growing, arching habit means established plants can reach 6–8 feet in a single warm season. The berries are toxic if eaten — a caution for households with small children or pets that might be attracted to the colorful fruits.

common_name: Purple Queen Bougainvillea (Rich Purple-Violet Bougainvillea Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; promote blooming by allowing slight water stress between waterings and by limiting nitrogen fertilizer — excess fertility and water promote vegetative growth at the expense of flower production.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Purple Queen is a bougainvillea cultivar producing bracts in rich, deep purple-violet to blue-purple tones — one of the more purely purple-toned bougainvilleas available, with bracts that lean toward violet-blue rather than the magenta-red of Barbara Karst or the scarlet of San Diego Red. The deeper, cooler purple tone has a different visual quality in the landscape — more sophisticated and less vivid than the hot pinks and reds, creating an elegant effect particularly effective with silver and gray foliage companions. Purple Queen has the same vigorous growth habit as other bougainvillea cultivars, capable of covering large wall areas or pergolas in favorable zone 9-11 conditions. The bloom response to humidity-following-drought, water stress promotion, and lean soil management applies equally to Purple Queen as to other bougainvillea cultivars — slightly stressing the plant consistently produces heavier flowering than well-fed and well-watered specimens. The purple bract color fades to mauve-pink as the flowers age on the plant, providing a naturally occurring color gradient from new vivid-purple bracts at the stem tips to older faded-mauve bracts closer to the main stems — an attractive multi-tone effect during extended bloom periods. Container-grown Purple Queen performs well on patios and in large planters where the root restriction actually promotes flowering.

common_name: Sweet Dani Lemon Basil (AAS-Winning Strongly Lemon-Scented Large Basil)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Sweet Dani is an excellent cut-and-come-again herb — regular harvest of stem tips prevents bolting and maintains vigorous leaf production through the growing season.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Sweet Dani is an All-America Selections award-winning lemon basil (Ocimum x citriodorum) recognized for its exceptional lemon fragrance, large plant size, and outstanding performance as both a culinary herb and ornamental annual. The intense lemon scent — produced by the citral compounds that distinguish lemon basil from sweet basil — is significantly stronger in Sweet Dani than in standard lemon basil varieties, filling a small garden area with citrus fragrance when brushed or harvested. The large, medium-green, slightly textured leaves have a complex flavor combining sweet basil aromatics with a bright, clean lemon note that is outstanding for lemon pasta sauces, fish preparations, lemon-herb vinaigrettes, herb teas, and potpourri. Sweet Dani is substantially larger than older lemon basil varieties — reaching 24–30 inches versus the compact 12–15 inches of some lemon basil selections — producing more harvestable leaf mass per plant. The plants bolt to flower in late summer in zone 9 heat but regular pinching of the growing tips delays this and extends the harvest season. The white flowers attract numerous bee species when allowed to bloom. Lemon basil provides a valuable flavor dimension distinct from standard sweet basil in herb garden collections and culinary applications where citrus brightness is desired.
